--- SOURCE 1: Climate change will slow U.S. crop yield growth by 2030 | EDF ---
URL: https://www.edf.org/climate-change-will-slow-us-crop-yield-growth-2030

SUMMARY:
Extreme heat, changing rainfall and other climate change impacts will make it harder to grow staple crops in the U.S. as soon as 2030. A new report from EDF — How Climate Change Will Impact U.S. Corn, Soybean and Wheat Yields: A county-level analysis of climate burdens and adaptation needs in the Midwest — uses an ensemble of 20 computer models to analyze how climate change will alter yields of staple crops in Iowa, Minnesota and Kansas by 2030 and 2050. The report models anticipated climate impacts down to 4,000 acres — smaller than county boundaries — to make the information as relevant and actionable as possible to farmers, policymakers, food companies and other agricultural decision-makers.

Climate change will slow U.S. crop yield growth by 2030
=======================================================

Corn, soybean and wheat harvests are vulnerable

 October 18, 2022 

*   [Share on Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.edf.org%2Fclimate-change-will-slow-us-crop-yield-growth-2030%3Futm_source%3Dfacebook%26utm_medium%3Dreferral%26utm_campaign%3Dedf_none_upd_web%26utm_id%3D1608060844)
*   [Share on Twitter](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.edf.org%2Fclimate-change-will-slow-us-crop-yield-growth-2030%3Futm_source%3Dtwitter%26utm_medium%3Dreferral%26utm_campaign%3Dedf_none_upd_web%26utm_id%3D1608060929&original_referer=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.edf.org%2Fclimate-change-will-slow-us-crop-yield-growth-2030&text=Climate%20change%20will%20slow%20U.S.%20crop%20yield%20growth%20by%202030&via=EnvDefenseFund&related=EnvDefenseFund)
*   [Share on LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/sharing/share-offsite/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.edf.org%2Fclimate-change-will-slow-us-crop-yield-growth-2030%3Futm_source%3Dlinkedin%26utm_medium%3Dreferral%26utm_campaign%3Dedf_none_upd_web%26utm_id%3D1608061071)
*   [Copy Link](https://www.edf.org/climate-change-will-slow-us-crop-yield-growth-2030#get-link)

![Image 12: View from above of trucks on farmland](https://www.edf.org/sites/default/files/2023-10/My-project-%282%29.png)

Extreme heat, changing rainfall and other climate change impacts will make it harder to grow staple crops in the U.S. as soon as 2030. Adaptation efforts must begin now to protect food supplies and farmer livelihoods.

A new report from EDF — [How Climate Change Will Impact U.S. Corn, Soybean and Wheat Yields: A county-level analysis of climate burdens and adaptation needs in the Midwest](https://www.edf.org/sites/default/files/2022-10/climate-impacts-midwest-crop-yields.pdf) — uses an ensemble of 20 computer models to analyze how climate change will alter yields of staple crops in Iowa, Minnesota and Kansas by 2030 and 2050.

[Download the full report](https://www.edf.org/sites/default/files/2022-10/climate-impacts-midwest-crop-yields.pdf)

[Explore how climate change will impact harvests in this interactive map](https://storymaps.arcgis.com/stories/3e8bf162733448179127b17f8c5b5cea)

By 2030:

*   Nearly all counties in Iowa will see corn yields that are more than 5% lower than they would have been without climate change. More than half will see declines of 10% or greater.
*   More than half of Minnesota counties will see soybean yields drop by more than 5% from what they would have been without climate change. Seventeen-percent will see declines of more than 10%.
*   Eight-percent of Kansas counties will see winter wheat yields drop by more than 5% from what they would otherwise be without climate change.

Warmer temperatures are a double-edged sword for crop productivity. In some counties, climate change will lead to more growing-degree days when conditions are favorable for crop growth. In all counties, climate change will increase killing-degree days when temperatures are too hot for crops to grow.

Those detrimental impacts far outweigh any boosts, and the resulting drag on yields could have devastating impacts for global food security and agricultural economies. The report used an optimistic climate scenario where pollution peaks by mid-century and then declines. The world isn’t on track to meet that goal, and consequently, reductions in yields could be much worse.

Solutions must be local
-----------------------

Agriculture has always been highly variable, with different soil types and water availability even within the same farm field. Climate impacts will be similar, with high variability between counties.

The report models anticipated climate impacts down to 4,000 acres — smaller than county boundaries — to make the information as relevant and actionable as possible to farmers, policymakers, food companies and other agricultural decision-makers.

Adaptation must begin now
-------------------------

Climate adaptation can ensure U.S. farms stay productive in the face of the climate change, continuing to support global food needs and rural economies.

Adaptation can be incremental or transformational. An incremental approach would include growing a more resilient variety of an existing crop on a farm. A transformational approach would include growing more diverse crop rotations across an entire region. We will need a mix of both.

Change at this scale will take time. Efforts to climate proof agriculture must begin as soon as possible.

[Learn more about advancing agricultural adaptation](https://www.edf.org/agriculture/climate-proofing-agriculture)

Abstract
--------

Climate change challenges efforts to maintain and improve crop production in many regions. In this Review, we examine yield responses to warmer temperatures, elevated carbon dioxide and changes in water availability for globally important staple cereal crops (wheat, maize, millet, sorghum and rice). Elevated CO 2 can have a compensatory effect on crop yield for C3 crops (wheat and rice), but it can be offset by heat and drought. In contrast, elevated CO 2 only benefits C4 plants (maize, millet and sorghum) under drought stress. Under the most severe climate change scenario and without adaptation, simulated crop yield losses range from 7% to 23%. The adverse effects in higher latitudes could potentially be offset or reversed by CO 2 fertilization and adaptation options, but lower latitudes, where C4 crops are the primary crops, benefit less from CO 2 fertilization. Irrigation and nutrient management are likely to be the most effective adaptation options (up to 40% in wheat yield for higher latitudes compared with baseline) but require substantial investments and might not be universally applicable, for example where there are water resource constraints. Establishing multifactor experiments (including multipurpose cultivar panels), developing biotic stress modelling routines, merging process-based and data-driven models, and using integrated impact assessments, are all essential to better capture and assess yield responses to climate change.

SUMMARY:
Climate change cuts global crop yields, even when farmers adapt | Stanford Doerr School of Sustainability *   After adjusting for how real farmers adapt, researchers estimate global yields of calories from staple crops in a high-emissions future will be 24% lower in 2100 than they would be without climate change. “When global production falls, consumers are hurt because prices go up and it gets harder to access food and feed our families,” said Solomon Hsiang, professor of environmental social sciences at the Stanford Doerr School of Sustainability and a senior author of the study. This research was supported as a project from the Climate Impact Lab, which received funding from the Carnegie Corporation; the Energy Policy Institute at the University of Chicago; the International Growth Centre; National Science Foundation (#SES143644); Sloan Foundation; Tata Center for Development; the Skoll Global Threats Fund; King Philanthropies; the Alfred P.

June 18, 2025

![Image 2: Aerial view of agricultural fields](https://sustainability.stanford.edu/sites/sustainability/files/styles/card_1900x950/public/media/image/gettyimages-2148055154_0.jpg?h=c7c84225&itok=FfJWnPyI)

Aerial view of agricultural fields. (Image credit: Getty Images)

In brief
--------

*   New research offers the most comprehensive look yet at how global crop yields are likely to change as the planet warms.
*   After adjusting for how real farmers adapt, researchers estimate global yields of calories from staple crops in a high-emissions future will be 24% lower in 2100 than they would be without climate change.
*   U.S. agriculture and other breadbaskets are among the hardest-hit in the study’s projections, while regions in Canada, China, and Russia may benefit.

The global food system faces growing risks from climate change, even as farmers seek to adapt, according to a June 18 study in [_Nature_](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-025-09085-w).

In contrast to previous studies suggesting that warming could increase global food production, the researchers estimate that every additional degree Celsius of global warming on average will drag down the world’s ability to produce food by 120 calories per person per day, or 4.4% of current daily consumption.

![Image 3: Solomon Hsiang portrait](https://sustainability.stanford.edu/sites/sustainability/files/styles/stanford_circle/public/media/image/solomonhsiang-portrait_0.jpeg.png?itok=TO4MOgqj)

Solomon Hsiang is a professor in the global environmental policy area of the Stanford Doerr School of Sustainability’s Department of Environmental Social Sciences. _(Image credit: Drew Bird)_

“When global production falls, consumers are hurt because prices go up and it gets harder to access food and feed our families,” said [Solomon Hsiang](https://profiles.stanford.edu/solomon-hsiang), professor of environmental social sciences at the [Stanford Doerr School of Sustainability](http://sustainability.stanford.edu/) and a senior author of the study. “If the climate warms by 3 degrees, that’s basically like everyone on the planet giving up breakfast.” That’s a high cost for a world where [more than 800 million people](https://www.who.int/news/item/24-07-2024-hunger-numbers-stubbornly-high-for-three-consecutive-years-as-global-crises-deepen--un-report) at times go a day or more without food because of inadequate access.

The projected losses for U.S. agriculture are especially steep. “Places in the Midwest that are really well suited for present day corn and soybean production just get hammered under a high warming future,” said lead study author [Andrew Hultgren](https://www.andrewhultgren.com/), an assistant professor of [agricultural and consumer economics](https://ace.illinois.edu/) at the [University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign](https://illinois.edu/). “You do start to wonder if the Corn Belt is going to be the Corn Belt in the future.”

Hsiang and Hultgren worked on the analysis with more than a dozen scholars over the past eight years as a project with [Climate Impact Lab](http://impactlab.org/), a research consortium that Hsiang co-directs with University of Chicago economist Michael Greenstone, Rutgers University climate scientist Robert Kopp, and climate policy expert Trevor Houser of the Rhodium Group.

“This is basically like sending our agricultural profits overseas. We will be sending benefits to producers in Canada, Russia, China. Those are the winners, and we in the U.S. are the losers,” said Hsiang. “The longer we wait to reduce emissions, the more money we lose.”

Limits to adaptation
--------------------

![Image 4: Maps depict projected end-of-century change in crop yields](https://sustainability.stanford.edu/sites/sustainability/files/styles/large/public/media/image/hultgren_maps_0.png?itok=57lwpfGP)

Projected end-of-century change in crop yields resulting from climate change in a scenario where emissions remain high, accounting for adaptation to climate and increasing incomes. _Credit: Adapted from Hultgren et al. (Nature, 2025)_

The study draws on observations from more than 12,000 regions across 55 countries. The team analyzed adaptation costs and yields for crops that provide two-thirds of humanity’s calories: wheat, corn, rice, soybeans, barley, and cassava.

Previous studies failed to account for realistic adaptation by farmers, assuming either “perfect” adaptation or none at all. The new study is the first to systematically measure how much farmers adjust to changing conditions. In many regions, for example, they switch crop varieties, shift planting and harvesting dates, or alter fertilizer use.

The team estimates these adjustments offset about one-third of climate-related losses in 2100 if emissions continue to rise, but the rest remain. “Any level of warming, even when accounting for adaptation, results in global output losses from agriculture,” said Hultgren.

The steepest losses occur at the extremes of the agricultural economy: in modern breadbaskets that now enjoy some of the world’s best growing conditions, and in subsistence farming communities relying on small harvests of cassava. In terms of food production capacity from staple crops, the analysis finds yield losses may average 41% in the wealthiest regions and 28% in the lowest income regions by 2100.

The modeling points to a 50% chance that global rice yields will increase on a hotter planet, largely because rice benefits from warmer nights, while the odds that yields will decline by century’s end range from roughly 70% to 90% for each of the other staple crops.

Higher emissions bring bigger losses
------------------------------------

![Image 5: Chart shows decline in global food production capacity as global surface temperatures rise. ](https://sustainability.stanford.edu/sites/sustainability/files/styles/large/public/media/image/hultgren_calories_0.png?itok=FhC0raNh)

Global warming exceeding 2 degrees Celsius above the 2001-2010 average would likely cut global food production capacity from six staple crops by nearly a quarter. _Credit: Adapted from Hultgren et al. (Nature, 2025)_

With the planet already about 1.5 degrees Celsius hotter than pre-industrial levels, farmers in many areas are experiencing longer dry spells, unseasonable heat waves, and erratic weather that undermines yields, even when inputs like fertilizer and water improve.

The study modeled future crop yields under a range of warming and adaptation scenarios. By 2100, the authors estimate global crop yields would be dragged down 11% if emissions rapidly plummet to net zero and 24% if emissions continue to rise unchecked.

In the shorter term, by 2050 the authors estimate climate change will drag global crop yields down by 8% – regardless of how much emissions rise or fall in the coming decades. That’s because carbon dioxide emissions stay in the atmosphere, trapping heat and causing damage for hundreds of years. “If we ignore those long-run damages, we assign an economic value of zero to them, and that is clearly wrong,” Hultgren said.

New tools aim to help policymakers target resources
---------------------------------------------------

Hsiang, Hultgren, and colleagues are now working to help governments make informed decisions about where to direct adaptation investments, recognizing that many farmers still lack access to even basic agricultural resources, such as better fertilizer and accurate weather data.

The team is [working with the United Nations Development Program](https://horizons.hdr.undp.org/) to disseminate the new climate risk insights to governments around the world and [developing a system](https://sustainability-accelerator.stanford.edu/quantifying-climate-vulnerability-and-adaptation-potential-decision-making-using-satellite-imagery) to identify communities most at risk of yield declines and where targeted support can be most effective. “We’re focusing on how to make it so that this is not actually what our future looks like, even if we can’t get our act together on the emissions side,” Hsiang said.

A favorable climate, he added, is a big part of what keeps farmland productive across generations. “Farmers know how to maintain the soil, invest in infrastructure, repair the barn,” Hsiang said. “But if you’re letting the climate depreciate, the rest of it is a waste. The land you leave to your kids will be good for something, but not for farming.”

Climate Change Impacts on Agriculture and Food Supply
=====================================================

There are nearly two million farms in the United States, and more than half the nation’s land is used for agricultural production.[1](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#1foot) The number of farms has been slowly declining since the 1930s,[2](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#2foot) though the average farm size has remained about the same since the early 1970s.[3](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#3foot)Many industries, such as food service and food manufacturing, are connected to agriculture and depend on farms.

Did You Know?

slide 2 of 5

*   ![Image 4: Flooded crop field](https://www.epa.gov/system/files/styles/gallery_medium/private/images/2022-11/iStock-1157251495-v2.jpg?h=e43589f0&itok=VKIBeIUv) 

**Soil erosion.** Heavy rainfalls can lead to more soil erosion, which is a major environmental threat to sustainable crop production.[41](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#41foot)

*   ![Image 5: Low water levels at Lake Mead](https://www.epa.gov/system/files/styles/gallery_medium/private/images/2022-11/iStock-1336921579-v2.jpg?h=f8d6b0a5&itok=Bk6KmZJz) 

**Drought.** Since early 2020, the U.S. Southwest has been experiencing one of the most severe long-term droughts of the past 1,200 years. Multiple seasons of record low precipitation and near-record high temperatures were the main triggers of the drought. [37](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#37foot)

*   ![Image 6: Firefighting helicopter putting out a fire](https://www.epa.gov/system/files/styles/gallery_medium/private/images/2022-11/iStock-612506686.jpg?h=6f8e8448&itok=GzlqT-B1) 

**Wildfires.** Some tribal communities are particularly vulnerable to wildfires due to their often-remote locations and lack of firefighting resources and staff.[38](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#38foot) In addition, because wildfire smoke can travel long distances from the source fire, its effects can be far reaching, especially for people with certain medical conditions or who spend long periods of time outside.

*   ![Image 7: Corn crops in a field](https://www.epa.gov/system/files/styles/gallery_medium/private/images/2022-11/iStock-1330585339.jpg?h=71976bb4&itok=9Ccqjd4I) 

**Decreased crop yields.** Rising temperatures and carbon dioxide concentrations may increase some crop yields, but the yields of major commodity crops (such as corn, rice, and oats) are expected to be lower than they would in a future without climate change.[39](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#39foot)

*   ![Image 8: Dairy cows in field](https://www.epa.gov/system/files/styles/gallery_medium/private/images/2022-11/iStock-906635620-v2.jpg?h=37d428da&itok=bCDgXVJP) 

**Heat stress.** Dairy cows are especially sensitive to heat stress, which can affect their appetite and milk production. In 2010, heat stress lowered annual U.S. dairy production by an estimated $1.2 billion.[40](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#40foot)

*   ![Image 9: Flooded crop field](https://www.epa.gov/system/files/styles/gallery_medium/private/images/2022-11/iStock-1157251495-v2.jpg?h=e43589f0&itok=VKIBeIUv) 

**Soil erosion.** Heavy rainfalls can lead to more soil erosion, which is a major environmental threat to sustainable crop production.[41](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#41foot)

*   ![Image 10: Low water levels at Lake Mead](https://www.epa.gov/system/files/styles/gallery_medium/private/images/2022-11/iStock-1336921579-v2.jpg?h=f8d6b0a5&itok=Bk6KmZJz) 

**Drought.** Since early 2020, the U.S. Southwest has been experiencing one of the most severe long-term droughts of the past 1,200 years. Multiple seasons of record low precipitation and near-record high temperatures were the main triggers of the drought. [37](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#37foot)

Agriculture is very sensitive to weather and climate.[4](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#4foot) It also relies heavily on land, water, and other natural resources that climate affects.[5](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#5foot) While climate changes (such as in temperature, precipitation, and frost timing) could [lengthen the growing season](https://www.epa.gov/climate-indicators/climate-change-indicators-length-growing-season) or allow different crops to be grown in some regions,[6](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#6foot) it will also make agricultural practices more difficult in others.

The effects of climate change on agriculture will depend on the rate and severity of the change, as well as the degree to which farmers and ranchers can adapt.[7](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#7foot) U.S. agriculture already has many practices in place to adapt to a changing climate, including crop rotation and [integrated pest management](https://www.epa.gov/ipm/introduction-integrated-pest-management). A good deal of research is also under way to help prepare for a changing climate.

The Link Between Agriculture and Climate Change
-----------------------------------------------

![Image 11: Cow in front of barn grazing](https://www.epa.gov/system/files/styles/small/private/images/2022-10/agriculture1%281%29.jpg?itok=vk1sEql3)

Climate change can affect crops, livestock, soil and water resources, rural communities, and agricultural workers. The agriculture sector also emits greenhouse gases into the atmosphere that contribute to climate change.

Read more about greenhouse gas emissions from agriculture and other sectors on the [Basics of Climate Change](https://www.epa.gov/climatechange-science/basics-climate-change)page.

For a more technical look at emissions from the agriculture sector, take a look at EPA's Greenhouse Gas Emissions Inventory [chapter on agriculture activities in the United States](https://www.epa.gov/ghgemissions/sources-greenhouse-gas-emissions#agriculture).

Learn how the agriculture sector is reducing [methane emissions](https://www.epa.gov/agstar/practices-reduce-methane-emissions-livestock-manure-management) from livestock waste through the [AgSTAR program](https://www.epa.gov/agstar/agstar-accomplishments).

Top Climate Impacts on Agriculture
----------------------------------

Climate change may affect agriculture at both local and regional scales. Key impacts are described in this section.

### 1. Changes in Agricultural Productivity

Climate change can make conditions better or worse for growing crops in different regions. For example, changes in temperature, rainfall, and frost-free days are leading to longer growing seasons in almost every state.[8](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#8foot)A longer growing season can have both positive and negative impacts for raising food. Some farmers may be able to plant longer-maturing crops or more crop cycles altogether, while others may need to provide more irrigation over a longer, hotter growing season.

Air pollution may also damage crops, plants, and forests.[9](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#9foot)For example, when plants absorb large amounts of ground-level ozone, they experience reduced photosynthesis, slower growth, and higher sensitivity to diseases.[10](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#10foot)

Climate change can also increase the threat of [wildfires](https://www.epa.gov/climate-indicators/climate-change-indicators-wildfires). Wildfires pose major risks to farmlands, grasslands, and rangelands.[11](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#11foot)

Temperature and precipitation changes will also very likely expand the occurrence and range of insects, weeds, and diseases.[12](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#12foot)This could lead to a greater need for weed and pest control.[13](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#13foot)

Pollination is vital to more than 100 crops grown in the United States.[14](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#14foot)Warmer temperatures and changing precipitation can affect when plants bloom and when [pollinators](https://www.fs.usda.gov/managing-land/wildflowers/pollinators), such as bees and butterflies, come out.[15](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#15foot)If mismatches occur between when plants flower and when pollinators emerge, pollination could decrease.[16](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#16foot)

Heat and humidity can also affect the health and productivity of animals raised for meat, milk, and eggs.[17](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#17foot)

### 2. Impacts to Soil and Water Resources

![Image 12: Oysters covered with barnacles](https://www.epa.gov/system/files/styles/small/private/images/2022-10/agriculture2.jpg?itok=hmv21BM_)

Oysters and other animals with limited mobility are vulnerable to hypoxia because they can’t move to other areas.[18](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#18foot)

Climate change is expected to increase the frequency of [heavy precipitation](https://www.epa.gov/climate-indicators/climate-change-indicators-heavy-precipitation) in the United States, which can harm crops by eroding soil and depleting soil nutrients.[19](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#19foot)

Heavy rains can also increase agricultural [runoff](https://www.epa.gov/nps/nonpoint-source-agriculture) into oceans, lakes, and streams which can harm water quality.[20](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#20foot)Runoff can carry nutrients, fertilizer, and pesticides into neighboring water bodies.

When coupled with warming water temperatures brought on by climate change, runoff can lead to depleted oxygen levels in water bodies. This is known as [hypoxia](https://www.epa.gov/ms-htf/hypoxia-101). Hypoxia can kill fish and shellfish. It can also affect their ability to find food and habitat, which in turn could harm the coastal societies and economies that depend on those ecosystems.[21](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#21foot)

Sea level rise and storms also pose threats to coastal agricultural communities. These threats include erosion, agricultural land losses, and saltwater intrusion, which can contaminate water supplies.[22](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#22foot)Climate change is expected to worsen these threats.[23](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#23foot)

### 3. Agricultural Workers' Health

Agricultural workers face several climate-related health risks. These include exposures to [heat](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-and-health-workers#heat) and other extreme weather, more [pesticide exposure](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-and-health-workers#pesticide) due to expanded pest presence, [disease-carrying pests](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-and-health-workers#insect)like mosquitos and ticks, and [degraded air quality](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-and-health-workers#respiratory).[24](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#24foot)Language barriers, lack of health care access, and other factors can compound these risks.[25](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#25foot)Higher temperatures and resulting heat stress are affecting farmworker safety and productivity, which can affect earnings and their own food security.[26](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#26foot)

Learn more about [climate change and the health of workers](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-and-health-workers).

For more specific examples of climate change impacts in your region, please see the [National Climate Assessment](https://toolkit.climate.gov/NCA5).

Agriculture and the Economy
---------------------------

![Image 13: Pie chart showing employment "U.S. employment in agriculture, food, and related industries, 2022"](https://www.epa.gov/system/files/styles/small/private/images/2025-01/agemploymentctewithfoodretail2022.png?itok=at3onrQm)

Agriculture contributed more than $1.53 trillion to the U.S. gross domestic product in 2023.[27](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#27foot)In 2022, the sector accounted for 10.4 percent of total U.S. employment.[28](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#28foot)These include not only on-farm jobs, but also jobs in food service and other related industries. Food service makes up the largest share of these jobs at 12.7 million.[29](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#29foot)

Cattle, corn, dairy products, and soybeans are the top income-producing commodities.[30](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#30foot)The United States is a key exporter of soybeans, other plant products, tree nuts, animal feeds, beef, and veal.[31](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#31foot)

[](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply)Population Impacts
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Many hired crop farmworkers are foreign-born people from Mexico and Central America.[32](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#32foot)Most hired crop farmworkers are not migrant workers; instead, they work at a single location within 75 miles of their homes.[33](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#33foot)Many hired farmworkers can be more at risk of climate health threats due to social factors, such as language barriers and health care access.

Climate change could affect food security for some households in the country. Most U.S. households are currently [food secure](https://www.ers.usda.gov/topics/food-nutrition-assistance/food-security-in-the-us/). This means that all people in the household have enough food to live active, healthy lives.In 2023, 13.5 percent (18.0 million households) were food insecure.[34](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#34foot)These households faced a lack of resources that resulted in difficulty providing enough food for all their members.U.S. households with above-average [food insecurity](https://health.gov/healthypeople/priority-areas/social-determinants-health/literature-summaries/food-insecurity) include those with an income below the poverty threshold, those headed by a single woman, and those with Black or Hispanic owners and lessees.[35](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#35foot)

Climate change can also affect food security for some Indigenous peoples in Hawai'i and other U.S.-affiliated Pacific islands. Climate impacts like sea level rise and more intense storms can affect the production of crops like taro, breadfruit, and mango.[36](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply#36foot) These crops are often key sources of nutrition and may also have cultural and economic importance.

What We Can Do
--------------

![Image 14: Monarch butterfly perched among yellow flowers](https://www.epa.gov/system/files/styles/small/private/images/2022-11/iStock-1210792722.jpg?itok=nBp2u8mv)

Planting a pollinator-attracting garden with native species is just one of [many ways to help pollinators](https://www.nps.gov/articles/000/pollinators-climateimpact.htm)like bees and butterflies thrive.

We can reduce the impact of climate change on agriculture in many ways, including the following:

*   **Incorporate climate-smart farming methods**. Farmers can use climate forecasting tools, plant cover crops, and [take other steps](https://www.climatehubs.usda.gov/) to help manage climate-related production threats.
*   **Join AgSTAR**. Livestock producers can get help in [recovering methane](https://www.epa.gov/agstar/learning-about-biogas-recovery), a potent greenhouse gas, from biogas created when manure decomposes.
*   **Reduce runoff**. Agricultural producers can strategically apply fertilizers, keep their animals out of streams, and [take more actions](https://www.epa.gov/nutrientpollution/sources-and-solutions-agriculture) to reduce nutrient-laden runoff.
*   **Boost crop resistance**. Adopt research-proven ways to reduce the impacts of climate change on crops and livestock, such as reducing pesticide use and improving pollination.
*   **Prevent food waste**. Stretch your dollar and shrink your carbon footprint by planning your [shopping trips carefully and properly storing food](https://www.epa.gov/recycle/preventing-wasted-food-home#toolkit). Donate nutritious, untouched food to food banks and those in need.

See additional actions you can take, as well as steps that companies can take, on EPA’s [What You Can Do About Climate Change](https://www.epa.gov/climate-change/what-you-can-do-about-climate-change-energy) page.

Related Climate Indicators
--------------------------

Learn more about some of the key indicators of climate change related to this sector from [EPA’s Climate Change Indicators](https://www.epa.gov/climate-indicators):

*   [Seasonal Temperature](https://www.epa.gov/climate-indicators/climate-change-indicators-seasonal-temperature)
*   [Drought](https://www.epa.gov/climate-indicators/climate-change-indicators-drought)
*   [Freeze-Thaw Conditions](https://www.epa.gov/climate-indicators/climate-change-indicators-freeze-thaw-conditions)
*   [Length of Growing Season](https://www.epa.gov/climate-indicators/climate-change-indicators-length-growing-season)
*   [Growing Degree Days](https://www.epa.gov/climate-indicators/climate-change-indicators-growing-degree-days)

*   [_Fifth National Climate Assessment_](https://toolkit.climate.gov/NCA5)_, Chapter 11: “Agriculture, Food Systems, and Rural Communities."_
*   [National Agricultural Center](https://www.epa.gov/agriculture/about-epas-national-agriculture-center). Provides agriculture-related news from all of EPA through a free email subscription service.
*   [U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) Economic Research Service](https://www.ers.usda.gov/). Produces research, information, and outlook products to enhance people’s understanding of agriculture and food issues.
*   [USDA Environmental Quality Incentives Program](https://www.nrcs.usda.gov/wps/portal/nrcs/main/national/programs/financial/eqip/). Provides financial and technical assistance to agricultural producers to address natural resource concerns.
*   [USDA Climate Hubs](https://www.climatehubs.usda.gov/). Connects farmers, ranchers, and land managers with tools to help them adapt to climate change impacts in their area.
*   [USDA Rural Development](https://www.rd.usda.gov/). Promotes economic development in rural communities. Provides loans, grants, technical assistance, and education to agricultural producers and rural residents and organizations.
*   [National Integrated Drought Information System](https://www.drought.gov/). Coordinates U.S. drought monitoring, forecasting, and planning through a multi-agency partnership. The [U.S. Drought Monitor](https://droughtmonitor.unl.edu/) assesses droughts on a weekly basis.
*   [Sustainable Management of Food](https://www.epa.gov/sustainable-management-food). Provides tools and resources for preventing and reducing wasted food and its associated impacts over the entire life cycle.
*   [Resources, Waste, and Climate Change](https://www.epa.gov/smm/resources-waste-and-greenhouse-gas-emissions). Learn how reducing waste decreases our carbon footprint and what business, communities, and individuals can do.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Increasing global temperatures can cause heat stress in crops, reducing yields and affecting growth cycles, especially for heat-sensitive species like corn and wheat.
Changes in rainfall distribution lead to droughts and floods, disrupting planting seasons and water availability, which directly impact crop productivity and soil health.
Warmer conditions and altered ecosystems promote pest proliferation, leading to higher infestations and increased reliance on pesticides, which can harm beneficial insects.
Projected yield reductions range from 3% to 25% for staple crops due to climate change, jeopardizing food security and increasing food prices globally.
Impacts of climate change vary significantly by region; some areas may experience improved conditions, while others, like the Midwest U.S., face severe yield declines.
